intro_band.agent_remit_summary: An interactive terminal coding/automation agent (the Deep Agents CLI, built on the deepagents SDK and LangGraph). It is meant to plan and carry out multi-step development tasks in a developer's local workspace under that developer's supervision — reading, searching and editing files, running shell commands via execute, tracking work with write_todos, spawning task subagents, and consulting external documentation through MCP servers declared in trusted .mcp.json files. High-impact actions are expected to be reviewable via human-in-the-loop approval, untrusted project MCP configs are expected to require explicit trust, and credentials are expected to stay out of the workspace and out of the logs.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python monorepo. The deepagents SDK (libs/deepagents/) builds a LangGraph agent in deepagents/graph.py (create_deep_agent, BASE_AGENT_PROMPT) with FilesystemMiddleware (allow/deny path ACLs, path-traversal validation), SubAgentMiddleware, a pluggable shell backend whose default LocalShellBackend (backends/local_shell.py) runs commands on the host with no sandbox, and an opt-in interrupt_on human-in-the-loop hook. The deepagents-cli package (libs/cli/) adds the MCP subsystem: mcp_tools.discover_mcp_configs() auto-discovers .mcp.json from the user home dir and the project root, mcp_trust.py gates project-level configs behind a SHA-256 fingerprint approval, mcp_auth.py runs OAuth device-code login and stores tokens 0600 under ~/.deepagents/.state/mcp-tokens/, and langchain-mcp-adapters exposes the servers' tools to the model. The repo's top-level .mcp.json declares two first-party HTTPS doc servers (docs-langchain, reference-langchain). Dependencies are range-pinned with committed uv.lock lockfiles; there is no durable local action log and no SECURITY.md.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is a capable harness whose strong primitives are opt-in while its riskiest path is the default. Deep Agents ships real controls — path-scoped filesystem permissions, a tested human-in-the-loop interrupt hook, a fingerprint trust gate for project MCP configs, careful 0600 credential storage — but none of the high-impact gates are on by default: the bundled shell execute tool uses an explicitly-unsandboxed LocalShellBackend, nothing requires approval before a write or a command, and the CLI's default_agent_prompt.md is a session-loaded notes file the agent is told it may rewrite. Combined with external content reaching the model unfiltered (file contents, shell output, MCP doc-server results), the default configuration is a one-hop persistence-and-execution chain (PRAX-2026-05-12-001).

On the MCP surface specifically the practical exposure today is low — the two declared servers are read-only first-party LangChain doc endpoints over TLS — but the structural gaps are real: no tool-poisoning check on tool descriptions or sanitization of tool outputs before they re-enter the model, no approval gate on MCP tool calls, and user-level .mcp.json files loaded with no trust prompt at all.

rules.rule_text: Help a developer accomplish coding and automation tasks in their local workspace — read/search/edit files, run shell commands, plan, spawn subagents, and consult declared MCP servers — under interactive developer supervision.
The agent may use read_file, write_file, edit_file, ls, glob, grep, execute, write_todos, task, and MCP tools loaded from trusted .mcp.json configs.

Outbound network is limited to: the configured LLM provider API; remote MCP servers declared in trusted .mcp.json files; LangSmith when tracing is enabled; and whatever the developer's own shell commands reach.

High-impact actions — shell command execution, file writes/deletes outside the working tree, and any MCP tool that sends/deletes/executes — should be gated by human-in-the-loop approval when the agent runs in a non-trivial context.
The agent MUST NOT load tools from an untrusted project-level .mcp.json without explicit operator approval (fingerprint trust).
When the agent processes untrusted input or runs in a non-trusted/multi-tenant/production context, the shell execute tool MUST use a sandboxed backend, not the unsandboxed LocalShellBackend.
Credentials (provider API keys, MCP OAuth tokens) MUST be stored with restrictive file permissions, never written into the workspace or version control, and never logged or printed.
File operations MUST reject path traversal and absolute-path escapes, and should be restrictable to an allowlist of paths.
The agent should maintain a durable, structured record of the actions it takes (tool invocations with parameters and timestamps) sufficient to reconstruct an incident.
The agent operates interactively under developer supervision; it MUST NOT run as an unattended background service.
Dependencies MUST be version-controlled via a committed lockfile and should be pinned to compatible ranges; the LLM model version should be specified.
Remote MCP servers MUST be reached over TLS.

External content that enters the agent's context (file contents, shell output, MCP tool results, fetched docs) should be treated as untrusted and not allowed to silently redirect the agent's behaviour.
The project should maintain a documented adversarial-testing / red-team practice and a published threat model (e.g. SECURITY.md), with findings feeding back into the agent's defaults.

findings.summary: Default CLI configuration chains unfiltered external content → an agent-writable session-notes file → unsandboxed shell exec with no approval, forming a one-hop persistence-and-execution chain.
findings.description: Each link is a default, not an edge case: file contents, shell output and MCP doc-server results enter the model context with no origin labeling or sanitization (PRAX-2026-05-12-006); default_agent_prompt.md is loaded into context and the agent is told it may rewrite it (PRAX-2026-05-12-004); and execute runs on the host through the unsandboxed LocalShellBackend with no per-command approval because human-in-the-loop is off by default (PRAX-2026-05-12-002, PRAX-2026-05-12-003). Injected instructions in any ingested content can therefore (a) be written into the notes file to persist across sessions and (b) drive arbitrary host commands. Mitigations exist and are each one configuration flag away — a sandbox backend, interrupt_on, scoped FilesystemPermission rules — but the shipped default engages none of them.
findings.policy_rule_text: High-impact actions — shell command execution, file writes/deletes outside the working tree, and any MCP tool that sends/deletes/executes — should be gated by human-in-the-loop approval when the agent runs in a non-trivial context. / When the agent processes untrusted input or runs in a non-trusted/multi-tenant/production context, the shell execute tool MUST use a sandboxed backend, not the unsandboxed LocalShellBackend. / External content that enters the agent's context (file contents, shell output, MCP tool results, fetched docs) should be treated as untrusted and not allowed to silently redirect the agent's behaviour.

evidence.snippet: module docstring: "It provides NO sandboxing or isolation - all operations run directly on the host machine with full system access."

"This file is for tracking project-specific context as you work. You can update this file to remember decisions, patterns, and context about this project." — a session-loaded file the agent is instructed it may rewrite
interrupt_on / InterruptOnConfig is an optional parameter to create_deep_agent(); no tool is gated unless the embedder sets it

recommended_actions: Ship the CLI with a non-permissive default: either a sandboxed shell backend (one of the BaseSandbox implementations) or interrupt_on covering execute, write_file/edit_file and task, so an out-of-the-box install does not present this chain.
Treat default_agent_prompt.md writes as a high-impact action (approval-gated) or make the file read-only to the agent and require an explicit user action to update it.
Label or fence external content (file contents, shell output, MCP tool results) when it re-enters the model context so injected instructions are distinguishable from operator intent.

findings.summary: The execute shell tool runs under the default LocalShellBackend, which executes commands on the host with no sandbox, no resource limits, and no per-command approval.
findings.description: The backend's own docstring states it "provides NO sandboxing or isolation" and lists production / multi-tenant / untrusted-input use as inappropriate, recommending an external sandbox or HumanInTheLoopMiddleware — but it is the default the SDK and CLI fall back to, and the project README's "shell access — execute for running commands (with sandboxing)" overstates the out-of-the-box posture. A sandboxed alternative (BaseSandbox and the LangSmith / Daytona / Modal / RunLoop implementations) exists; nothing enforces or defaults to it.
findings.policy_rule_text: When the agent processes untrusted input or runs in a non-trusted/multi-tenant/production context, the shell execute tool MUST use a sandboxed backend, not the unsandboxed LocalShellBackend.

evidence.snippet: "`LocalShellBackend`: Filesystem backend with unrestricted local shell execution. ... It provides NO sandboxing or isolation - all operations run directly on the host machine with full system access."
"Shell access — `execute` for running commands (with sandboxing)" — sandboxing is not the default; it requires a sandbox backend or HITL

recommended_actions: Default the CLI's shell backend to a sandboxed implementation, or refuse to enable execute on the unsandboxed backend without an explicit opt-in flag.
Reword the README so "with sandboxing" reflects what's available rather than what's on by default.

findings.summary: Human-in-the-loop approval (interrupt_on / HumanInTheLoopMiddleware) is available and tested but off by default; nothing gates filesystem writes, shell exec, subagent spawns, or MCP tool calls unless the embedder configures it.
findings.description: create_deep_agent() accepts an interrupt_on / InterruptOnConfig with per-tool decision rules, the config propagates to subagents, and the behaviour is covered by tests/integration_tests/test_hitl.py — a genuine, working approval mechanism. But it is opt-in: a default agent or default CLI session runs every tool, including execute, without a confirmation step.
findings.policy_rule_text: High-impact actions — shell command execution, file writes/deletes outside the working tree, and any MCP tool that sends/deletes/executes — should be gated by human-in-the-loop approval when the agent runs in a non-trivial context.

evidence.snippet: interrupt_on is an optional kwarg of create_deep_agent(); InterruptOnConfig supports per-tool allowed_decisions but is unset by default
HITL interrupt payload shape, decision configs and post-resume tool messages are tested — the mechanism works; it is just not enabled by default

recommended_actions: Enable interrupt_on for high-impact tools (execute, write_file, edit_file, task, send/delete MCP tools) by default in the CLI, with an explicit flag to relax it.
Document the recommended interrupt_on configuration prominently in the SDK quickstart.

findings.summary: default_agent_prompt.md is a session-loaded "project notes" file the agent is explicitly told it may rewrite without approval — a persistence surface, since file contents and MCP doc output reach the model context.
findings.description: The file's own text addresses the agent ("You can update this file to remember decisions, patterns, and context about this project"), so it is part of the agent's runtime context and the agent has standing instruction to write it; write_file/edit_file are available with no approval by default. Content that the agent ingests from files it reads or from MCP doc servers can therefore steer a write into this file that then re-enters context on the next session. It is not version-controlled per session and not change-audited.
findings.policy_rule_text: External content that enters the agent's context (file contents, shell output, MCP tool results, fetched docs) should be treated as untrusted and not allowed to silently redirect the agent's behaviour.

evidence.snippet: "This file is for tracking project-specific context as you work. You can update this file to remember decisions, patterns, and context about this project."
the agent's file tools (read/write/edit) operate on the workspace with no per-write approval by default; the notes file is a writable path

recommended_actions: Require explicit user confirmation before the agent writes default_agent_prompt.md (or any session-loaded notes/memory file), or make it append-only with a visible diff.
Keep a change log / version history for the notes file so a poisoning write is auditable after the fact.

findings.summary: No durable, structured local audit log of tool invocations (filesystem ops, shell exec, subagent spawns, MCP tool calls); observability relies on optional LangSmith tracing and an in-memory session counter.
findings.description: The CLI keeps a SessionStats object (request count, token totals, wall time) in memory for the current run, and the SDK can trace to LangSmith when LANGSMITH_API_KEY is set; the MCP session manager logs invocations "internally" but does not persist them. There is no on-disk, structured, action-level record by default, so an incident cannot be reconstructed from the agent's own output.

tags.label: All tool invocations logged with identity and parameters

findings.policy_rule_text: The agent should maintain a durable, structured record of the actions it takes (tool invocations with parameters and timestamps) sufficient to reconstruct an incident.

evidence.snippet: SessionStats accumulates request/token/wall-time counters in memory for the current session — not an action log and not persisted
tracing path exists via LangSmith but is opt-in (off unless LANGSMITH_API_KEY / tracing is configured)

recommended_actions: Write a structured local action log (JSON lines: timestamp, tool name, parameters with secrets redacted, result status) for every tool invocation, on by default.
Make the MCP session manager's per-invocation records persist to that log rather than staying in memory.

findings.summary: MCP tools are loaded from declared servers with no tool-poisoning check on tool descriptions and no sanitization of tool outputs before they re-enter the model context.
findings.description: load_mcp_tools() / langchain-mcp-adapters surface whatever tools and descriptions a server advertises directly to the model, and tool results (e.g. documentation pages fetched from docs.langchain.com / reference.langchain.com) flow back into context unfiltered. A tool description containing instruction-like language, or a doc page carrying an injected instruction, would be an indirect-injection vector. The practical risk is bounded today because the two declared servers are first-party LangChain HTTPS endpoints, but the check is structurally absent.

tags.label: Tool descriptions contain no hidden model instructions
All tool outputs sanitized before returning to model

findings.policy_rule_text: External content that enters the agent's context (file contents, shell output, MCP tool results, fetched docs) should be treated as untrusted and not allowed to silently redirect the agent's behaviour.

evidence.snippet: two HTTP MCP servers declared: docs-langchain (https://docs.langchain.com/mcp), reference-langchain (https://reference.langchain.com/mcp) — their tools and outputs are exposed to the model as-is
load_mcp_tools()/_discover_tools() return the server's advertised tools; no scan of tool descriptions for instruction-like language and no sanitization of tool result content

recommended_actions: Scan MCP tool descriptions for instruction-like language at load time and warn (or refuse) when found.
Treat MCP tool results as untrusted external content — fence or label them when they re-enter the model context.

findings.summary: MCP tool calls are invoked with no approval checkpoint unless interrupt_on is configured; a trusted server that exposed a send/delete/exec tool would have it invoked without confirmation.
findings.description: MCP tools loaded via the adapter are ordinary tools in the agent's tool set, so they inherit the default no-approval posture (PRAX-2026-05-12-003). The two declared servers expose only read-only documentation lookups, so today's exposure is low; the gap is that the trust gate covers loading a config, not invoking a high-risk tool from one.
findings.policy_rule_text: High-impact actions — shell command execution, file writes/deletes outside the working tree, and any MCP tool that sends/deletes/executes — should be gated by human-in-the-loop approval when the agent runs in a non-trivial context.

evidence.snippet: loaded MCP tools are added to the agent's tool list; tool filtering supports fnmatch name-exclusion but there is no per-call approval hook specific to MCP tools
declared servers expose read-only doc lookups; nothing prevents a future trusted server from advertising a send/delete/exec tool

recommended_actions: Default to interrupt_on for MCP tools, or at least for any MCP tool whose name/schema suggests a write, send, delete or exec action.
Surface the full tool list a server contributes (with descriptions) at trust-prompt time so the operator approves capabilities, not just a config file.

findings.summary: User-level MCP configs (~/.mcp.json, ~/.deepagents/.mcp.json) are auto-loaded and trusted with no fingerprint or approval gate — only project-level .mcp.json files are gated.
findings.description: discover_mcp_configs() reads ~/.deepagents/.mcp.json, ~/.mcp.json, /.deepagents/.mcp.json and /.mcp.json; classify_discovered_configs() sends only the project-level ones through the trust store, and mcp_trust.py exists specifically because project configs may contain stdio servers that execute local commands. A user-home-dir config (which can also declare stdio command-executing servers) is loaded with no prompt — so anything that can write a file in the user's home directory can inject MCP servers into every Deep Agents session.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent MUST NOT load tools from an untrusted project-level .mcp.json without explicit operator approval (fingerprint trust).

evidence.snippet: discovery paths include ("~/.deepagents/.mcp.json", "user-level"), ("~/.mcp.json", "user-level") — classify_discovered_configs treats user-level configs as trusted; only project-level configs go through the trust store
module docstring: "Manages persistent approval of project-level MCP configs that contain stdio servers (which execute local commands)." — user-level configs are out of scope of the trust check

recommended_actions: Apply the same fingerprint trust gate to user-level .mcp.json files (or at minimum prompt on first sight of a stdio/command server from any source).
Show which discovered configs are being loaded, and from where, at session start.

findings.summary: The agent's domain is unrestricted by default — a general-purpose shell + filesystem + subagent harness with no topic or scope gate; scoping primitives exist but are not applied out of the box.
findings.description: create_deep_agent() with the default backend gives the model file read/write/edit, glob/grep, host shell execute, and task subagent spawning with no constraint on what it works on. Scoping is possible — FilesystemMiddleware allow/deny path ACLs, fnmatch tool-name exclusion for MCP tools, named subagent profiles — but the CLI applies none of them by default, and there is no egress restriction on either execute or (once trusted) an MCP HTTP server URL.
findings.policy_rule_text: Outbound network is limited to: the configured LLM provider API; remote MCP servers declared in trusted .mcp.json files; LangSmith when tracing is enabled; and whatever the developer's own shell commands reach.

evidence.snippet: create_deep_agent() wires read_file/write_file/edit_file/ls/glob/grep/execute/write_todos/task with no domain restriction; restriction is the embedder's job
FilesystemMiddleware supports FilesystemPermission(operations, paths, mode) allow/deny rules — a real scoping primitive that is not populated by default

recommended_actions: Ship the CLI with a sensible default FilesystemPermission rule set (e.g. allow reads/writes under the project root, deny outside it) rather than wide-open.
Offer a documented "restricted" profile (no host execute, project-only file access) and make switching to it a one-flag operation.

findings.summary: No SECURITY.md, threat model, or documented adversarial-testing program; control behaviour is well unit-/integration-tested but there is no injection/jailbreak/red-team test suite.
findings.description: The repo tests its controls thoroughly — tests/unit_tests/test_permissions.py exercises filesystem permission enforcement, tests/integration_tests/test_hitl.py exercises the approval interrupt, and ACP's tests/test_dangerous_patterns.py exercises shell-injection pattern detection — which is real evidence of adversarial thinking driving the design of those controls. What's missing for a higher score is a dedicated adversarial test suite (prompt injection, tool poisoning, jailbreak), a published threat model / SECURITY.md, and any record of red-team findings changing the architecture.
findings.policy_rule_text: The project should maintain a documented adversarial-testing / red-team practice and a published threat model (e.g. SECURITY.md), with findings feeding back into the agent's defaults.

evidence.snippet: tests detection of command substitution, backticks, ${VAR}/$VAR expansion, process substitution, here-docs, redirects and background ops — a real injection-pattern control with tests behind it
absent — no published threat model, security policy, or disclosure process at the repo root

recommended_actions: Add a SECURITY.md with the threat model (host RCE via execute, context poisoning, MCP supply chain) and a disclosure process.
Add an adversarial test suite: prompt-injection-in-file-content, tool-poisoning-in-MCP-description, notes-file-poisoning round-trip — and wire the results back into defaults.

findings.summary: MCP OAuth tokens are stored as plaintext JSON files under ~/.deepagents/.state/mcp-tokens/ (0600, 0700 dir, atomic write, never logged) rather than an OS keychain or secret vault.
findings.description: mcp_auth.py handles this carefully — os.open with O_EXCL and mode 0o600, a follow-up chmod for Windows, an atomic temp-file-then-replace write, a 0o700 parent directory, and a docstring explicitly forbidding logging the token via %r/str()/f-strings. It is a reasonable approach. It is still below the OWASP MCP minimum-bar item "secrets stored in a vault, not in files" — a macOS Keychain / libsecret / Windows Credential Manager backend would close that gap.

tags.label: Secrets stored in vault, not in files

findings.policy_rule_text: Credentials (provider API keys, MCP OAuth tokens) MUST be stored with restrictive file permissions, never written into the workspace or version control, and never logged or printed.

evidence.snippet: fd = os.open(str(tmp), flags, 0o600) ... parent dir chmod 0o700 ... tokens written under ~/.deepagents/.state/mcp-tokens/<server>.json; docstring: never log a token via %r/str()/f-string

recommended_actions: Offer an OS-keychain token-storage backend (macOS Keychain / libsecret / Windows Credential Manager) and prefer it when available, falling back to the current 0600-file store.

findings.summary: libs/deepagents/pyproject.toml leaves langsmith unbounded (>=0.8.3) and wcmatch with no version spec; the committed uv.lock makes installs reproducible but a plain pip install could resolve a future-major langsmith.
findings.description: The committed uv.lock files for both packages mean uv sync / CI installs are reproducible, and the CLI pins its internal dependency exactly (deepagents==0.6.1) with a Dependabot config in place. The residual gap is the SDK's pyproject.toml dependency specs: langsmith>=0.8.3 has no upper bound and wcmatch has no version constraint at all, so a non-locked install (e.g. pip install deepagents into a fresh env) could pull an incompatible future release.
findings.policy_rule_text: Dependencies MUST be version-controlled via a committed lockfile and should be pinned to compatible ranges; the LLM model version should be specified.

evidence.snippet: dependency specs include langsmith>=0.8.3 (no upper bound) and wcmatch (no version spec); other deps are range-pinned (e.g. langchain-core>=1.4.0,<2.0.0)

recommended_actions: Add upper bounds: langsmith>=0.8.3,<1.0.0 and a compatible range for wcmatch in libs/deepagents/pyproject.toml.

findings.summary: DEEPAGENTS_CLI_<NAME> environment variables are allowed to shadow canonical provider env vars (precedence: DEEPAGENTS_CLI_<NAME> > <NAME>).
findings.description: _env_vars.py centralises the CLI's environment-variable registry and a drift test enforces it; the precedence rule lets a DEEPAGENTS_CLI_-prefixed variable override the canonical one (e.g. an API key or base URL). This is operator-controlled and intentional, but worth noting: a process-environment foothold could redirect provider/API configuration without touching any file.
findings.policy_rule_text: Outbound network is limited to: the configured LLM provider API; remote MCP servers declared in trusted .mcp.json files; LangSmith when tracing is enabled; and whatever the developer's own shell commands reach.

evidence.snippet: central registry of DEEPAGENTS_CLI_* vars; documented precedence DEEPAGENTS_CLI_<NAME> > <NAME> allows shadowing canonical env vars

recommended_actions: No action required; if surfacing it, document that DEEPAGENTS_CLI_-prefixed overrides take precedence so operators can reason about effective config.

positives.title: Filesystem path-safety + allow/deny ACLs
positives.description: FilesystemMiddleware validates every path (rejects '..', requires absolute paths, rejects '~') unconditionally, and supports FilesystemPermission allow/deny rules per operation and path pattern — a real, runnable Zero-Trust primitive on the file tools.

positives.title: Fingerprint trust gate for project MCP configs
positives.description: Project-level .mcp.json files (which may declare stdio servers that execute local commands) must be approved via a SHA-256 fingerprint stored in ~/.deepagents/config.toml before their tools load — an explicit operator-in-the-loop checkpoint for the MCP supply chain.

positives.title: Disciplined credential handling
positives.description: API keys (auth_store.py → ~/.deepagents/.state/auth.json) and MCP OAuth tokens (mcp_auth.py → mcp-tokens/*.json) are written 0600 in 0700 directories via atomic temp-file-then-replace, with explicit docstring discipline against ever logging credential values; no hardcoded secrets were found in the repo.

positives.title: Working, tested human-in-the-loop interrupt
positives.description: create_deep_agent()'s interrupt_on / InterruptOnConfig supports per-tool approval decisions, propagates from parent to subagents, and is covered by integration tests — the approval mechanism exists and works; the gap is that it is not on by default.

positives.title: Reproducible dependency builds
positives.description: Both packages commit uv.lock lockfiles, the CLI pins its internal dependency exactly (deepagents==0.6.1), the default model version is specified (claude-sonnet-4-6), and .github/dependabot.yml automates dependency updates.

positives.title: Shell dangerous-pattern detection (ACP)
positives.description: The ACP layer detects command-injection patterns — command substitution, backticks, variable expansion, process substitution, here-docs, redirects, background operators — and the detection is unit-tested.

positives.title: Declared MCP servers use TLS and are first-party
positives.description: The repo's top-level .mcp.json declares only two HTTPS servers, both LangChain-operated documentation endpoints (docs.langchain.com, reference.langchain.com); the config loader validates transport types.

log_files.no_logs_note: No durable, structured action log was found in the codebase; observability relies on optional LangSmith tracing (off unless LANGSMITH_API_KEY is configured) and an in-memory SessionStats counter — see PRAX-2026-05-12-005.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. Deep Agents has the building blocks of a defensible posture — filesystem path ACLs and traversal checks that run unconditionally, a tested human-in-the-loop interrupt mechanism, a fingerprint trust gate for project MCP configs, disciplined credential handling, committed lockfiles and Dependabot — which keeps it well clear of the bottom of the scale. What holds it at Partial is that the controls protecting the highest-impact capability (shell execution) are not engaged by default: the default backend is unsandboxed, approval is opt-in, external content enters the model context unfiltered, and there is no durable local audit trail. Supply-chain hygiene is the strongest dimension; continuous monitoring is the weakest.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: A general-purpose shell + filesystem + subagent harness with no topic or scope gate by default; scoping primitives exist — FilesystemMiddleware path allow/deny ACLs, fnmatch MCP tool-name exclusion, named subagent profiles — but the CLI applies none of them out of the box (PRAX-2026-05-12-009).

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: External content — file contents, shell output, MCP tool results, fetched LangChain docs — enters the model context with no origin labeling or sanitization, and the CLI's writable default_agent_prompt.md is itself a session-loaded surface (PRAX-2026-05-12-004, -006); the practical risk is bounded today only because the declared MCP servers are first-party.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Real interposition exists and some of it runs unconditionally — FilesystemMiddleware validates every path and the project-MCP fingerprint trust gate is enforced — but the shell execute tool runs unsandboxed on the host with no per-command policy and human-in-the-loop approval is off by default, so the highest-impact capability is ungated (PRAX-2026-05-12-001, -002, -003).

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Both packages ship committed uv.lock lockfiles, the CLI pins its internal dep exactly (deepagents==0.6.1), the default model version is specified, .github/dependabot.yml automates updates, and no credentials live in the repo; loose specs for langsmith and wcmatch in libs/deepagents/pyproject.toml are the only gap (PRAX-2026-05-12-012).

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: Control behaviour is genuinely exercised — tests/unit_tests/test_permissions.py, tests/integration_tests/test_hitl.py, and ACP's tests/test_dangerous_patterns.py test the permission system, the approval interrupt, and shell-injection detection — but there is no adversarial/injection/jailbreak test suite, no SECURITY.md, and no record of red-team findings driving design changes (PRAX-2026-05-12-010).

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: No durable, structured record of the agent's actions: tool invocations are not logged locally; observability depends on optional LangSmith tracing (off unless LANGSMITH_API_KEY is set) and an in-memory SessionStats counter (PRAX-2026-05-12-005).
